ontology.foodex2_description: Fish cake — processed fish product made from minced fish paste, starch and seasoning, formed into a cake shape
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for spicy fishcake as a distinct food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed fish snacks is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of spicy fishcake is one piece weighing approximately 50 g, commonly eaten as a snack, added to noodle soups, or served alongside rice dishes in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Fishcake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Fishcake (cr_id F03040014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish cake / processed fish product analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore spicy fishcake in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the closest analogous processed fish cake entry in the University of Sydney GI Database, which reports GI values for starch-bound fish paste products in the range 48–56. Glycemic load per 50 g serving calculated as: (52 × 12.1 × 0.50) / 100 = 3.1 g. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1095 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid spicy fishcake; even a single 50 g piece delivers approximately 548 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the 1500 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (12.1 g/100 g) with 2.0 g dietary fibre and 8.5 g protein per 100 g; estimated low GI (~52) supports moderate glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy fishcake is moderately suitable for type 2 diabetes management when portion-controlled to one piece (50 g); the protein and fibre content slow gastric emptying, but the high sodium content poses an indirect cardiovascular risk that is clinically relevant for diabetic patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 1095 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular risk driver; processed fish product with unknown saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of spicy fishcake due to its extremely high sodium content, which promotes fluid retention, elevated blood pressure, and increased cardiac workload; choose fresh, unsalted fish preparations instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy fishcake (辣味鱼饼; Kek ikan pedas) is a processed Singaporean fish snack made from minced fish paste combined with wheat flour or tapioca starch, diced fresh chilli, and salt, then formed into a flat cake shape and steamed or lightly fried. It is a common street food and hawker-centre item in Singapore, frequently served as a standalone snack, skewered on satay sticks, added to fishball noodle soup, or eaten alongside rice. The texture is firm and slightly springy, with a mild fish flavour and a noticeable chilli heat. Per 100 g, it provides 112 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 3.3 g fat, and 12.1 g carbohydrate with 2.0 g dietary fibre. The most clinically significant feature is its very high sodium content of 1095 mg per 100 g, which makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 52 (low), making it moderately acceptable for blood glucose management when portion size is controlled. Calcium (32 mg/100 g) and iron (0.8 mg/100 g) are present in modest amounts. The product is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and contains fish and gluten allergens.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in fishcake?
Is fishcake good for high blood pressure?
What is spicy fishcake made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040014) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is the only element reducing full certainty on the GI sub-field; overall record confidence remains high.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03040014 — this processed fishcake formulation uses no added sugar and the fish paste base contains negligible intrinsic sugars; confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this product. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for spicy fishcake (F03040014) as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the closest processed fish cake entry; a direct measurement for Singapore spicy fishcake is not available. Halal status is typical for commercially produced fishcakes in Singapore but individual product labels should be verified. Chilli content may be a concern for individuals with nightshade sensitivity or rheumatoid arthritis.
